name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا۟ عِبَادًا لِّى مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن كُونُوا۟ رَبَّـٰنِيِّـۧنَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
**It does not behoove any human that Allah should give him the Book, judgement and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah.’ Rather [he would say], ‘Be a godly people, because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it.’** [Qarai: 3:79] (79)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ أَرْبَابًا ۗ أَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
**And he would not command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords. Would he call you to unfaith after you have submitted [to Allah]?** [Qarai: 3:80] (80)
```

## (Commentary)

The occurrence of these verses immediately following those related to the matter of Jesus (عليه السلام) indicates that they serve as a second chapter in the argument establishing the innocence of the Messiah from what the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) among the Christians believe concerning him. The discourse is as if to say: He is not as you claim; he is neither a Lord (رب), nor did he claim Lordship (الربوبية) for himself. As for the first point: it is because he was a created human being (مخلوق بشري) whom his mother carried, gave birth to, and raised in the cradle, except that he had no father, just like Adam (عليهما السلام); thus, his likeness with Allah is as the likeness of Adam. As for the second point: it is because he was a prophet (نبي) who was given the Book (الكتاب), judgment (الحكم), and prophethood (النبوة). A prophet of such standing does not exceed the bounds of servitude (العبودية) nor divests himself of the raiment of slavery (الرقية). How then could it be possible for him to say to the people, "Take me as a lord and be servants to me instead of Allah"? Or how could such a thing be permissible for any other servant of Allah, whether angel (ملك) or prophet, such that he would grant a servant of Allah what is not his by right, or deny a prophet what Allah has established for him of the Message (الرسالة), thereby taking from him a right that belongs to him?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما كان لبشر أن يؤتيه الله الكتاب والحكم والنبوة ثم يقول للناس كونوا عباداً لي من دون الله** — *It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgement and prophethood, and then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah.’* (3:79). "Human" (البشر) is a synonym for "man" (الإنسان), and it is applied to both the individual and the collective; thus, a single person is a human (بشر) just as a group of them is human (بشر).

His saying, "It is not for a human" (ما كان لبشر), the letter *lam* (اللام) denotes possession (الملك), meaning: he does not possess that right, or it is not his by right. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **مَّا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّتَكَلَّمَ بِهَٰذَا** — *It is not for us to say such a thing* (24:16), and His saying: **وَمَا كَانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَن يَغُلَّ** — *A prophet may not illicitly take from the spoils* (3:161).

His saying, the Exalted, "that Allah should give him the Book, judgment, and prophethood" (أن يؤتيه الله الكتاب والحكم والنبوة) is the subject of "was" (كان), yet it serves as a preamble for what follows in His saying, "then he should say to the people" (ثم يقول للناس). Mentioning this preamble—despite the meaning being apparently sound without it—serves another facet of the meaning of "It is not for a human." For if it were said, "It is not for a human to say to the people," it would mean that this right was not legislated for him, even if it were possible for him to say it out of wickedness (فسقاً) or rebellion (عتواً). However, when it is said: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Book, judgment, and prophethood, then he should say," it means that Allah’s granting him knowledge (العلم) and understanding (الفقه) from His presence, and His nurturing him with a Lordly upbringing (تربية ربانية), would never allow him to exceed the bounds of servitude (العبودية), nor would it permit him to dispose of what he does not own or have a right to. This is as the Exalted recounts from Jesus (عليه السلام) in His saying: **وَإِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لي أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ** — *And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say].’* (5:116).

From here, the subtle point (النكتة) in His saying, "that Allah should give him..." (أن يؤتيه الله) becomes clear, rather than saying, "It is not for a human to whom Allah gave the Book... to say..." The second phrasing would imply the meaning of the origin of legislation (أصل التشريع) as previously mentioned, whereas His saying, "that Allah should give him..." implies that such a thing is absolutely impossible. That is, the Lordly upbringing and Divine guidance (الهداية الالهية) never fail to achieve their purpose, as the Exalted said: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ** — *They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood. So if these [people] disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them.* (6:89).

The sum of the meaning is that it is not possible for a human to combine these Divine blessings with calling people to worship himself—by being given the Book, judgment, and prophethood, and then saying to the people, "Be my servants instead of Allah." Thus, the verse, according to its context (السياق), is in one respect like His saying, the Exalted: **لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — *The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him].* — until He said — **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ اسْتَنكَفُوا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فَيُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا** — *As for those who disdain and are arrogant, He will punish them with a painful punishment, and they will not find besides Allah any guardian or helper.* (4:172-173). What is understood from the verse is that the Messiah, as well as the angels brought near, are too exalted in status and too high in rank to disdain the worship of Allah, for disdaining His worship necessitates a painful punishment; and far be it from Allah to punish His noble prophets and His near angels.

**If you say:** The use of "then" (ثم), which denotes a delay (التراخي), in His saying, "then he should say to the people," contradicts the combination (الجمع) you mentioned.

**I say:** What we mentioned regarding the meaning of combination is the summarized meaning. Just as it is correct to consider the gathering and accompaniment between things united in time, it is also correct to consider it between things that are ordered and sequential; thus, it is a type of combination (الجمع).

As for His saying: "Be servants to me instead of Allah" (كونوا عباداً لي من دون الله), the word "servants" (العباد) like "slaves" (العبيد) is the plural of "servant/slave" (عبد). The difference between them is that "servants" (العباد) is predominantly used when attributed to Allah, the Glorified—one says "servants of Allah" (عباد الله)—and one does not say "servants of people" (عباد الناس), but rather "slaves of people" (عبيد الناس). The qualification of His saying, "servants to me" with His saying, "instead of Allah" (من دون الله) is a necessary qualification, for Allah, the Glorified, does not accept any worship except that which is sincere for His noble countenance, as the Exalted said: **أَلَا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخَالِصُ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِن دُونِهِ أَوْلِيَاءَ مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِي مَا هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كَاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ** — *Now, surely, sincere religion belongs to Allah. As for those who take guardians besides Him, [saying,] ‘We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah,’ Allah will indeed judge between them concerning that about which they differ. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate.* (39:3). Thus, He rejected the worship of anyone who worships another alongside Him, even under the pretext of seeking nearness, mediation, or intercession (الاستشفاع).

Furthermore, the reality of worship (العبادة) is not realized except by granting some degree of independence (استقلال) to the object of worship, even in the form of polytheism (الإشراك); for a partner, insofar as he is a partner, is a participant possessing some independence. Allah, the Glorified, possesses absolute Lordship (الربوبية المطلقة), so His Lordship is not complete nor is His worship sound except with the negation of independence from everything in every respect. Therefore, worshipping other than Allah is worshipping him "instead of Allah," even if Allah is worshipped alongside him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولكن كونوا ربانيين بما كنتم تعلمون الكتاب وبما كنتم تدرسون** — *Rather [he would say], ‘Be Lordly (ربانيين) — *those intensely devoted to the Lord in knowledge and worship* — because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it.’* (3:79). The "Lordly" (الرباني) is one attributed to the Lord (الرب); the letters *alif* and *nun* were added to denote magnification (التفخيم), just as one says "bearded" (لحياني) for one with a large beard, and the like. Thus, the meaning of "Lordly" is one who is intensely specialized in the Lord and frequently occupied with His servitude and worship. The "by" (الباء) in His saying, "because of what you were" (بما كنتم) denotes causality (السببية), and "what" (ما) is an infinitive particle (مصدرية). The speech implies a suppressed "he says," and the meaning is: "But he says: Be Lordly because of your teaching the Book to the people and your studying it among yourselves."

"Studying" (الدراسة) is more specific than "teaching" (التعليم), as it is mostly used for what is learned from a book by reading it. Al-Raghib said: "The house was studied (درس)" means its traces remained, and the remaining of traces implies their eventual effacement in themselves; therefore, *durūs* (الدروس) was explained as effacement. Likewise, "he studied the book," and "I studied the knowledge" means I took its traces by memorization. Since taking that occurs through constant reading, constant reading was expressed as memorization. The Exalted said: **وَدَرَسُوا مَا فِيهِ** — *and they have studied what is in it* (7:169), and He said: **بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ الْكِتَابَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ** — *because of your teaching the Book and because of your studying it* (3:79), and **وَمَا آتَيْنَاهُم مِّن كُتُبٍ يَدْرُسُونَهَا** — *We have not given them any books that they might study* (34:44). [End of quote].

The sum of the discourse is that a human of this standing only calls you to clothe yourselves with faith (الإيمان) and certainty (اليقين) in what is in the Book—which you teach and study—of the principles of Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية), and to be characterized by and realize the virtuous traits and ethics it contains, and to act upon the righteous deeds (الصالحات) to which you call the people, so that through this you may devote yourselves entirely to your Lord and become Lordly scholars (علماء ربانيين) through Him.

His saying, "because of what you were" (بما كنتم), since it contains the past tense denoting realization (التحقق), is not devoid of some indication that the discourse in the verse is intended as an allusion (التعريض) to the Christians among the People of the Book regarding their claim that Jesus informed them that he was His son and His word—according to the differences in interpreting sonship. This is because the Children of Israel were the ones who held a Heavenly Book in their hands, teaching it and studying it, yet they differed concerning it with a disagreement accompanied by alteration (التغيير) and distortion (التحريف). Jesus (عليه السلام) was only sent to clarify for them some of that wherein they differed, and to make lawful some of what had been forbidden to them; in short, to call them to fulfill the obligatory duties of teaching and studying, which is to be Lordly in their teaching and studying of the Book of Allah, the Glorified.

Although the verse does not resist being applied to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in some respect—as his call also concerned the People of the Book who were teaching and studying the Book of Allah—Jesus (عليه السلام) is more prior in its application to him, and his mission was specifically for the Children of Israel, unlike the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

As for the other great prophets among the "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم) and the Book, such as Noah, Abraham, and Moses, the content of the verse does not apply to them [in this specific context of allusion], and this is evident.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أو يأمركم أن تتخذوا الملائكة والنبيين أرباباً** — *Nor would he command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords.* (3:80). This is conjoined to His saying, "he says" (يقول), according to the well-known recitation which puts "command you" (يأمركم) in the subjunctive case. This is because a group of the People of the Book, like the Sabians, used to worship the angels and attribute that to a religious call; and like the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) who used to say that the angels are the daughters of Allah, while claiming they were following the religion of Abraham (عليه السلام)—this concerns taking the angels as lords.

As for taking the prophets as lords, it is like the saying of the Jews: "Ezra is the son of Allah," as recounted by the Quran, though Moses (عليه السلام) never permitted that for them, nor does anything occur in the Torah except the monotheism (التوحيد) of the Lord. Had he permitted that for them, it would have been a command to it—far be it from him to do so.

The two verses—namely His saying, "then he should say to the people, ‘Be my servants instead of Allah’" and His saying, "Nor would he command you to take the angels and the prophets for lords"—differ in two respects regarding their context: First, the one commanded in the first ("then he should say to the people") is "the people," while in the second, it is those addressed by the verse. Second, the thing commanded in the first is servitude (العبودية) to him, while in the second, it is taking them as lords (أرباباً).

As for the first: since the discourse was intended as an allusion to the Christians in their worship of Jesus and their explicit claim of his divinity (ألوهيته), attributing that to his call, it was an attribution from them to him that he said: "Be servants to me." This is unlike taking the angels and prophets as lords in the sense said of others besides Jesus, for that contradicts divinity by its implication (بلازمه), not by its explicit statement (بصريحه); therefore, it was said "lords" (أرباباً) and not "gods" (آلهة).

As for the second: the reason is that both expressions ("Be servants to me" and "he commands you to take") are commands that, if they were to pertain to anyone, would pertain to those addressed by these verses among the People of the Book and the Arabs. However, since the expression in the first verse used "saying" (القول), and saying necessitates direct address (المشافهة), and those present at the time of the verse's revelation were not present then, it was necessarily said: "then he should say to the people," and it was not said: "then he should say to you." This is unlike the word "command" (الأمر) used in the second verse, for it does not necessitate direct address but is complete even with absence (الغيبة); for a command pertaining to the ancestors pertains to the descendants while maintaining national unity. As for "saying," because it conveys—according to its common usage—the hearing of a voice, it necessitates direct address and presence, unless it is intended to mean merely the sense of conveying understanding (التفهيم).

Accordingly, the basis in the context of these verses is presence and collective address, as followed in His saying, the Exalted: "Nor would he command you..." to the end of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أيأمركم بالكفر بعد إذ أنتم مسلمون** — *Would he command you to disbelieve after you have submitted [to Allah]?* (3:80). The apparent address is that it pertains to all who affiliate themselves with prophethood among the People of the Book or those claiming a connection to the prophets, as the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance claimed they were *Hunafā’* (حنفاء). The discourse is based on a hypothetical premise (الفرض والتقدير); the meaning is that, assuming you respond to this human who was given the Book, judgment, and prophethood, you would be submitters (مسلمين) to Allah, adorned with the ornament of Islam and dyed with its hue. How then could it be possible for him to command you to disbelieve (الكفر) and lead you astray from the path to which he guided you by the permission of Allah, the Glorified?

From here, it becomes clear that what is meant by Islam (الإسلام) is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), which is the religion of Allah according to all the prophets. This is also indicated by the surrounding verses carrying this meaning of Islam—namely His saying, the Exalted, previously: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ** — *Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam* (3:19), and His saying, the Exalted, afterwards: **أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ اللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ** — *Do they seek a religion other than the religion of Allah?* — until He said — **وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ الْإِسْلَامِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ** — *Should anyone follow a religion other than Islam, it shall never be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter.* (3:85).

Some exegetes have mentioned that what is meant by His saying, the Exalted, "It is not for a human that Allah should give him..." to the end of the two verses, is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—based on what was narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول). Its summary is: Abu Rafi‘ al-Qurazi and a man from the Christians of Najran said to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Do you want us to worship you, O Muhammad?" So Allah sent down: "It is not for a human that Allah should give him..." to the end of the two verses. Then he supported this with His saying at their end, "after you have submitted [to Allah]," for Islam is the religion brought by Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The flaw in this is that it confuses Islam in the usage of the Quran—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) with which all prophets were sent—and Islam as a technical term (الاصطلاح) that arose among Muslims after the era of revelation; the discussion on this has already preceded.

## (A Conclusion Containing Several Sections)

## 1 - What is the story of Jesus and his mother in the Quran?

The mother of the Messiah was Mary, the daughter of ‘Imran. Her mother conceived her and vowed to make what was in her womb, when she delivered it, consecrated (محرراً) to serve the Temple, assuming that what was in her womb was male. When she delivered her and it became clear to her that it was female, she was saddened and felt regret. Then she named her Mary, meaning "the servant." Her father ‘Imran had died before her birth, so she brought her to the Temple to hand her over to the priests, among whom was Zechariah. They disputed over her guardianship, then they agreed on drawing lots. The lot fell to Zechariah, so he took charge of her until she reached maturity. Then she struck a veil between herself and them; she used to worship Allah, the Glorified, therein, and no one entered upon her except Zechariah. Whenever Zechariah entered the sanctuary (المحراب) to see her, he found provision with her. He said, "O Mary, from where does this come to you?" She said, "It is from Allah; indeed Allah provides for whomever He wishes without stint." She (peace be upon her) was a truthful woman (صديقة), and she was protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, pure (طاهرة), chosen (مصطفاة), and spoken to (محدثة)—the angels spoke to her: that Allah had chosen her and purified her, and she was of the obedient (القانتين) and one of the signs of Allah for all the worlds (العالمين). (Surah Al ‘Imran 3:35-44, Surah Maryam 19:16, Surah al-Anbiya’ 21:91, Surah al-Tahrim 66:12).

Then Allah the Exalted sent the Spirit (الروح) to her while she was veiled, and he appeared to her as a harmonious man (بشراً سوياً). He mentioned to her that he was a messenger from her Lord to grant her, by Allah’s permission, a son without a father. He gave her glad tidings of the manifest miracles that would appear from her son, and informed her that Allah would support him with the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), and teach him the Book, wisdom, the Torah, and the Gospel, and make him a messenger to the Children of Israel with clear signs. He informed her of his status and story, then he breathed the Spirit into her, and she conceived him as a woman conceives her child. (Verses from Al ‘Imran: 35-44).

Then Mary withdrew with him to a distant place, and the birth pangs brought her to the trunk of a palm tree. She said, "I wish I had died before this and been a thing forgotten, out of mind!" Then he called her from below her, "Do not grieve! Your Lord has made a rivulet below you. Shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself; it will drop fresh ripe dates upon you. So eat and drink and be consoled. Then if you see any human, say, ‘Indeed I have vowed a fast to the All-Beneficent, so I will not speak to any human today.’" Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. (Surah Maryam 19:20-27). His gestation, birth, speech, and all other affairs of his existence were of the same nature as those of other human individuals.

When her people saw her—in this state—they rose against her with slander and blame, as evidenced by the state of a woman who has conceived and given birth without a husband. They said, "O Mary, you have certainly brought a monstrous thing! O sister of Aaron, your father was not an evil man, nor was your mother unchaste." She pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is a child in the cradle?" He said, "Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet. He has made me blessed wherever I may be, and He has enjoined upon me prayer and charity as long as I live, and to be dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a tyrant, wretched. Peace be to me the day I was born, and the day I die, and the day I am raised alive." (Surah Maryam 19:27-33). This speech from him (peace be upon him) was like a brilliant prelude (براعة الاستهلال) to what he would undertake in opposing transgression and injustice, reviving the law of Moses (عليه السلام) and rectifying it, renewing what had been effaced of its gnosis, and clarifying that wherein they differed regarding its signs.

Then Jesus (عليه السلام) grew up and reached youth. He and his mother followed the customary course of human life, eating and drinking, and within them was what is in all other people of the accidents of existence (عوارض الوجود) until the end of their lives.

Then Jesus (عليه السلام) was given the Message (الرسالة) to the Children of Israel, so he set out calling them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), saying: "I have come to you with a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay the form of a bird, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s permission. And I will heal the blind and the leper and I will revive the dead by Allah’s permission. And I will inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you. Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him."

He used to call them to his new law (شريعة), which was a confirmation of the law of Moses (عليه السلام), except that he abrogated some of what had been forbidden in the Torah as a hardship upon the Jews. He used to say: "I have come to you with wisdom and to clarify for you some of that wherein you differ." And he used to say: "O Children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah and bringing glad tidings of a messenger who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad."

He (peace be upon him) performed what he had mentioned to them of miracles, such as creating the bird, reviving the dead, healing the blind and the leper, and informing of the unseen (المغيبات) by Allah’s permission.

He continued to call them to the monotheism of Allah and his new law until he despaired of their faith, having witnessed the rebellion of the people, their stubbornness, and the arrogance of the priests and rabbis toward that. So he chose from the small group that believed in him the Disciples (الحواريين) as his helpers toward Allah.

Then the Jews rose against him, intending to kill him, but Allah took him (توفاه الله) and raised him to Himself. It was made to appear so to the Jews: some claimed they killed him, and some claimed they crucified him, but it was only made to appear so to them. (Al ‘Imran 3:45-58, al-Zukhruf 43:63-65, al-Saff 61:6 and 14, al-Ma’idah 5:110 and 111, al-Nisa’ 4:157 and 158). This is the summary of what the Quran recounts concerning Jesus son of Mary and his mother.

## 2 - The status of Jesus with Allah and his position in himself:

He (peace be upon him) was a servant of Allah and he was a prophet (Surah Maryam 19:30). He was a messenger to the Children of Israel (Al ‘Imran 3:49). He was one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم), the possessor of a law and a Book, which is the Gospel (al-Ahzab 33:7, al-Shura 42:13, al-Ma’idah 5:46). Allah named him the Messiah, Jesus (Al ‘Imran 3:45). He was a word from Allah and a spirit from Him (al-Nisa’ 4:171). He was an Imam (al-Ahzab 33:7). He was among the witnesses over deeds (al-Nisa’ 4:159, al-Ma’idah 5:117). He was a bringer of glad tidings of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) (al-Saff 61:6). He was distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near (Al ‘Imran 3:45). He was among the chosen (Al ‘Imran 3:33). He was among the elect, and he was among the righteous (al-An‘am 6:85-87). He was blessed wherever he was, and he was pure (زكياً), and he was a sign for the people and a mercy from Allah, and dutiful to his mother, and peace was upon him (Maryam 19:19-33). He was among those whom Allah taught the Book and wisdom (Al ‘Imran 3:48). These are twenty-two characteristics from the stations of guardianship (مقامات الولاية) which comprise the sum of how Allah described this noble prophet and raised his rank. They are of two types: acquired (اكتسابية), such as servitude, nearness, and righteousness; and specialized (اختصاصية). We have explained each of them in its appropriate place in this book to the extent of our understanding, so let one refer to their respective places.

## 3 - What did Jesus (peace be upon him) say? And what was said about him?

The Quran mentions that Jesus was a servant and a messenger, and that he did not claim for himself what they attributed to him, nor did he speak to them except with the Message; as the Exalted said: **وَإِذْ قَالَ اللَّهُ يَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ قَالَ سُبْحَانَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِي نَفْسِي وَلَا أَعْلَمُ مَا فِي نَفْسِكَ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ ، مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَا أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنتَ أَنتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ ، قَالَ اللَّهُ هَٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنفَعُ الصَّادِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ** — *And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary, did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]. Had I said it, You would certainly have known it: You know whatever is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self. Indeed You are the Knower of the Unseen. I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.’’ And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things. If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ Allah will say, ‘This is a day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness.’* (5:116-119).

This wondrous speech, which contains the very essence of servitude (العبودية) and includes the most eloquent of manners (الأدب), expresses what Jesus the Messiah (عليه السلام) saw as his own position regarding the Lordship of his Lord, and toward the people and their deeds. He mentioned that he saw himself, in relation to his Lord, as a servant who has no affair except compliance; he does not refrain except by command, nor does he act except by command. He was not commanded except to call to the worship of Allah alone, and he did not say to them except what he was commanded: "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord."

He had nothing to do with the people except to bear witness (الشهادة) over their deeds; as for what Allah does with them and to them on the day they return to Him, he has no part in that, whether He forgives or punishes.

**If you say:** Then what is the meaning of what preceded in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة): that Jesus (عليه السلام) is among the intercessors on the Day of Resurrection, who will intercede and whose intercession will be accepted?

**I say:** The Quran is explicit, or nearly explicit, in that. The Exalted said: **وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — *Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it].* (43:86). And the Exalted said concerning him: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا** — *and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them* (4:159). And the Exalted said: **وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَالتَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنجِيلَ** — *and when I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel* (5:110). The discussion on the meaning of intercession has already been exhaustively presented. This is different from the "atonement" (التفدية) which the Christians believe in—which is the nullification of recompense through ransom and compensation—for that nullifies the absolute Divine sovereignty (السلطنة المطلقة الإلهية), as will be explained. The verse only negates that [atonement]. As for intercession, the verse does not address its matter, neither by affirmation nor negation; for if it were in the process of affirming it—despite its incompatibility (1) with the context—the appropriate speech would have been: "and if You forgive them, indeed You are the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful." And if it were in the process of negating it, there would be no reason to mention the witness over the people. This is a summary of what will come in the detailed interpretation of the verses, Allah willing.

As for what people said concerning Jesus (عليه السلام), although they split into various sects after him and differed in their paths—to the point that they may have exceeded seventy in terms of the general categories of their differences, while the specific sects and opinions are very many indeed.

However, the Quran only concerns itself with what they said regarding the matter of Jesus himself and his mother, because of its connection to the foundation of monotheism (التوحيد), which is the sole purpose of what the Quran and the upright primordial religion (الدين الفطري) call toward. As for some specifics like the issue of distortion (التحريف) and the issue of atonement (التفدية), it did not concern itself with them to that same extent.

What the Quran recounts from them or attributes to them is in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَى الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ** — *and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’* (9:30), and what is in its meaning, like His saying: **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ** — *And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!* (21:26). And in His saying: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** — *They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’* (5:72). And in His saying: **لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ** — *They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity.’* (5:73). And in His saying: **وَلَا تَقُولُوا ثَلَاثَةٌ** — *and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’* (4:171).

These verses, although they apparently contain different words with varying contents and meanings—and therefore have sometimes been interpreted (2) as referring to the differences of sects, such as the Melkites who believe in literal sonship, the Nestorians who believe that the descent and sonship are like the radiance of light upon a transparent body like crystal, and the Jacobites who believe it is by way of transformation (الانقلاب), and that the Deity, the Glorified, transformed into flesh and blood.

The proof for this is the unity of the argument (الاحتجاج) brought against them in the Quranic language.

To clarify: the Torah and the current Gospels all explicitly state the monotheism of the Deity, the Exalted, on one hand, while the Gospel explicitly states sonship on the other, and states that the Son is the Father and none other.

They did not interpret the sonship found therein as being for honor (التشريف) or blessing (التبريك), despite there being instances of explicit statement of that, such as his saying: "But I say to you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who persecute you and mistreat you, so that you may be sons of your Father who is in heaven; for He makes His sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust. For if you love those who love you, what reward do you have? Do not even the tax collectors do the same? And if you greet only your brothers, what more are you doing than others? Do not even the Gentiles do the same? Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect." (End of the fifth chapter of the Gospel of Matthew) (1).

And his saying also in prayer: "Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name," etc.

These words and their like that occurred in the Gospel are what called the Christians to the belief in the Trinity (التثليث) within unity.

What is meant by it is to preserve "that the Messiah is the son of Allah" while maintaining the monotheism (التوحيد) which the Messiah stipulated in his teaching, as in his saying: "The most important is, 'Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.'" (Gospel of Mark - Chapter Twelve).

The summary of what they believe (even if it does not result in a rational conclusion) is: that the Essence (الذات) is one substance (جوهر) having three persons (أقانيم). What is meant by "person" (الاقنوم) is the attribute (الصفة) which is the mode of a thing's appearance, manifestation, and self-disclosure to another, and the attribute is not other than the described. The three persons are: the person of Existence (الوجود), the person of Knowledge (العلم)—which is the Word—and the person of Life (الحياة)—which is the Spirit.

These three persons are: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The first is the person of Existence, the second is the person of Knowledge and the Word, and the third is the person of Life. Thus, the Son—who is the Word and the person of Knowledge—descended from his Father—who is the person of Existence—accompanied by the Holy Spirit—who is the person of Life by which things are illuminated.

Then they differed in the interpretation of this summary with a great difference that necessitated their fragmentation and branching into many sects and paths exceeding seventy, and the news of them will come to you to the extent that suits the nature of this book.

If you reflect on what we have presented, you will know: that what the Quran recounts from them, or attributes to them in His saying: "and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah,’" and His saying: "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary,’" and His saying: "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’" and His saying: "and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Desist!"—all of that returns to one meaning (which is the Trinity of Unity) that is common among all the modern sects in Christianity, and it is what we presented in the meaning of the Trinity of Unity.

He restricted Himself to this common meaning because that which refutes their sayings regarding the Messiah (عليه السلام) specifically—despite their number and fragmentation—is one matter that refutes them in a single manner, as will become clear.

## 4 - The Quran's argument against the doctrine of the Trinity

The Quran, in its argument, refutes the claim of the Trinitarians through two paths: First, the general path, which is the demonstration of the impossibility of a son (الابن) for Him, the Exalted, in Himself—that is, whether Jesus was the son or someone else. Second, the specific path, which is the demonstration that Jesus son of Mary is not a divine son, but rather a created servant.

As for the first path, its clarification is that the reality of sonship (البنوة) and procreation (التولد) is that one of these living material beings—like humans, animals, or even plants—separates a part of its own substance, then makes it, through gradual nurturing, another individual of its kind similar to itself, upon whom the properties and effects that were upon the source part are established. For example, the animal separates from itself the sperm, and the plant separates from itself the pollen, then it begins its gradual nurturing until it becomes another animal or plant similar to itself. It is well-known that this is impossible for Allah, the Glorified: **Firstly**, because it necessitates material corporeality (الجسمية المادية), and Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above matter and its needy concomitants such as motion (الحركة), time (الزمان), space (المكان), and so on. **Secondly**, because Allah, the Glorified, due to the absolute nature of His Divinity and Lordship, possesses absolute self-subsistence (القيومية المطلقة) over all else; everything besides Him is needy in its existence to Him, subsisting in its existence through Him. How then can one assume something other than Him that matches Him in kind, is independent of Him in itself, and possesses the essence, attributes, and laws that He possesses without being needy of Him? **Thirdly**, because the permissibility of begetting and procreation for Him, the Exalted, would necessitate the permissibility of gradual action (الفعل التدريجي) for Him, the Exalted, which would necessitate His falling under the law of matter and motion, which is a contradiction; rather, what occurs by His will and volition, the Exalted, occurs without delay or gradualness.

This demonstration is what is conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ بَل لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** — *And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is.* (2:116-117). According to what we have explained, His saying, "Immaculate is He" (سبحانه) is a proof; His saying, "to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him" is another proof; and His saying, "the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides..." is a third proof.

It is possible to consider His saying, "the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (بديع السموات والأرض) as the addition of an attribute to its agent, and to understand from it that His creation, the Exalted, is without a prior model; thus, procreation is impossible from Him because it would be creation according to the model of Himself—since their assumption is identity (العينية)—so this phrase would be another proof.

Even if we assume their saying, "Allah has taken a son" was spoken not literally but metaphorically regarding the meaning of son and child—meaning the separation of something from something that matches it in reality without material division or temporal gradualness (and this is what the Christians aim for in their saying, "the Messiah is the son of Allah" after refining it)—to escape thereby the problem of corporeality, matter, and gradualness, the problem of similarity (المماثلة) remains.

To clarify: establishing the Son and the Father is necessarily establishing number, which is establishing real multiplicity (الكثرة الحقيقية), even if specific unity (الوحدة النوعية) is assumed between the Father and the Son—like the father and son among humans are one in human reality, yet many insofar as they are two individuals of man. Accordingly, if the unity of the Deity is assumed, everything besides Him—including the Son—would be other than Him, owned by Him, and needy of Him; thus, the assumed Son would not be a Deity like Him. And if a Son matching Him is assumed, not needy of Him but independent like Him, monotheism (التوحيد) regarding the Deity, exalted is His Name, would be nullified.

This demonstration is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَقُولُوا ثَلَاثَةٌ انتَهُوا خَيْرًا لَّكُمْ إِنَّمَا اللَّهُ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ سُبْحَانَهُ أَن يَكُونَ لَهُ وَلَدٌ لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ ، وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا** — *and do not say, ‘[God is] a trinity.’ Desist! It is better for you. Indeed Allah is but one God. Immaculate is He that He should have a son. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and Allah suffices as trustee.* (4:171).

As for the second path, which is the demonstration that the person of Jesus son of Mary (عليه السلام) is not a son of Allah participating with Him in the Divine reality, it is because of what was in him of humanity (البشرية) and its concomitants.

To clarify: the Messiah (عليه السلام) was carried by Mary, she nurtured him as a fetus in her womb, then she delivered him as a woman delivers her child, then she raised him as a child is raised in the care of his mother. Then he began to grow and pass through the stages of life and progress in the levels of age from childhood, youth, and middle age. In all of that, his state was the state of a natural human in his life; he was subject to the accidents and states that subject a human: hunger and satiety, joy and sorrow, pleasure and pain, eating and drinking, sleep and wakefulness, fatigue and rest, and so on.

This is what was witnessed of the state of the Messiah (عليه السلام) during his stay among the people. No rational person doubts that one whose state is this is a human like other humans of his kind. If he is so, then he is a created, made being (مخلوق مصنوع) like all other individuals of his kind. As for the occurrence of wonders and the realization of miracles at his hand—such as reviving the dead, creating the bird, and healing the blind and the leper—as well as the realization of wonders of signs in his existence—such as his formation without a father—these are only matters that break the familiar habit and the current law in nature; for they are rare in existence, not impossible. Here is Adam, whom the Heavenly Books mention was created from dust and had no father; and here are the prophets of Allah like Salih, Abraham, and Moses (عليهم السلام), at whose hands many miraculous signs occurred, mentioned in the records of revelation, without that necessitating divinity in them, nor a departure from the bounds of humanity.

This method is the one followed in His saying, the Exalted: **لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ** — *They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’ while there is no god except the One God.* — until He said — **مَّا الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ الطَّعَامَ انظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ الْآيَاتِ ثُمَّ انظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ** — *The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly [other] apostles have passed before him. His mother was a truthful woman. Both of them used to eat food. Behold how We clarify the signs for them, then behold how they are perverted!* (5:73-75). He specifically mentioned "eating food" from among all actions because it is among the best of them in indicating materiality (المادية) and necessitating need and poverty (الحاجة والفاقة) which are incompatible with divinity. For it is well-known that one who hungers and thirsts by his nature, then is satiated by a meal or has his thirst quenched by a drink, possesses nothing but need and poverty which none but another can remove. What is the meaning of divinity for one of this standing? For he who is surrounded by need and requires something external to himself to remove it is deficient in himself, managed by another; he is not a God, self-sufficient in His essence, but rather a managed creature (مخلوق مدبر) under the Lordship of the one to whom his management returns.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, can return: **لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — *They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’ To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them. He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things.* (5:17).

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the previously quoted verse (verse 75), addressing the Christians: **قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَاللَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** — *Say, ‘Do you worship besides Allah what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm, while Allah is the All-hearing, the All-knowing?’* (5:76).

For the criterion in this type of argument is that what was witnessed of the Messiah's affair is that he lived according to the law current in human life, characterized by all the specific human attributes, actions, and states—such as eating, drinking, and all other human needs and human properties. This being characterized and characterized was not according to the outward sense or the deception of imagination only, but was in reality; the Messiah (عليه السلام) was a human possessing these attributes, states, and actions. The Gospels are filled with his naming himself a human and the Son of Man, and full of stories speaking of his eating, drinking, sleeping, walking, traveling, fatigue, speaking, and the like, such that none of them accepts diversion or interpretation. With the admission of these matters, what applies to others applies to the Messiah; he does not possess anything from another, just like others, and he can be destroyed just like others.

Likewise, the matter of his worship and his prayer is such that there is no doubt that what he performed of worship was only for nearness to Allah and submission to the sanctity of His presence, not for teaching the people or for other purposes similar to that.

To the matter of worship and arguing with it, His saying, the Exalted, points: **لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَن يَكُونَ عَبْدًا لِّلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ وَمَن يَسْتَنكِفْ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَيَسْتَكْبِرْ فَسَيَحْشُرُهُمْ إِلَيْهِ جَمِيعًا** — *The Messiah would never disdain to be a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near [to Him]. And whoever disdains His worship and is arrogant, He will soon muster them all toward Him.* (4:172). The worship of the Messiah is the first proof that he is not a God, and that divinity belongs to another in which he has no share. For what is the meaning of a thing placing itself in the station of servitude and being owned by itself? And a thing being self-subsistent from the very same respect by which it subsists itself? The matter is clear. Likewise, the worship of the angels reveals that they are not the daughters of Allah, the Glorified, nor is the Holy Spirit a God, since they were all worshippers of Allah, obedient to Him, as the Exalted said: **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ** — *And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, they are [His] honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. He knows that which is before them and that which is behind them, and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased, and they are apprehensive for fear of Him.* (21:26-28).

Furthermore, the Gospels are filled with the fact that the Spirit is obedient to Allah and His messengers, complying with the command and subject to the judgment; and there is no meaning to a thing commanding itself, nor to its obedience to its own essence, nor to its submission and compliance to its own creature.

Similar to the Messiah's worship of Allah, the Glorified, in indicating the distinction (المغايرة) is his calling the people to the worship of Allah, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ ، وَقَالَ الْمَسِيحُ يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَاهُ النَّارُ وَمَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ** — *They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ But the Messiah said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers shall not have any helpers.’* (5:72). The path of the verse and its argument is manifest.

The Gospels are also replete with his call to Allah, the Glorified. Although they do not contain this comprehensive expression: **اعبدوا الله ربي وربكم** — "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord," they do include the call to the worship (العبادة) of Allah, and his acknowledgment that He is his Lord in Whose hand is the rein of his affairs, and his acknowledgment that He is the Lord of the people. His call does not explicitly include the worship of himself even once, despite what they contain of his saying: "I and my Father are one" (Gospel of John, Chapter 10). It is necessary, assuming its authenticity, to interpret it as meaning: "My obedience is the obedience of Allah," as the Exalted said in His Noble Book: **مَّن يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطَاعَ اللَّهَ** — *Whoever obeys the Apostle has certainly obeyed Allah* (4:80).

## 5 — The Messiah is among the intercessors with Allah and is not a redeemer:

The Christians claimed that the Messiah redeemed them with his noble blood, and for this reason, they titled him "the Redeemer" (الفادي). They said: When Adam disobeyed Allah by eating from the forbidden tree in Paradise, he erred thereby and sin (الخطيئة) became attached to him; likewise, it became attached to his progeny after him as they procreated and reproduced. The recompense for sin is punishment in the Hereafter and eternal perdition (الهلاك الأبدي) from which there is no escape. Yet Allah, the Glorified, was Merciful and Just.

At that point, a difficult and insoluble problem arose: if He were to punish Adam and his progeny for their sin, it would contradict His mercy (الرحمة) for which He created them; and if He were to forgive them, it would contradict His justice (العدل), for the requirement of justice is to punish the criminal sinner for his crime and sin, just as its requirement is to reward the virtuous obedient one for his goodness and his [avoidance of] evil. (1)

This difficulty remained as it was until it was resolved through the blessing of the Messiah. This occurred by the Messiah (who is the Son of God, and is God Himself) entering a single womb from the progeny of Adam—that of Mary the Virgin—and being born from her as a human is born. Thus, he was a perfect man because he was the son of a man, and a perfect God because he was the Son of God—and the Son of God is God (Exalted is He)—infallible (معصوماً) from all sins and errors.

After he lived among the people for a brief period of time, socializing and mingling with them, eating and drinking with them, speaking to them and finding comfort in them, and walking among them, he was surrendered to his enemies so they might kill him with the most evil

of deaths, which is the death of crucifixion (الصلب), the victim of which is cursed in the Divine Book. Thus, he bore the curse and the crucifixion with its attendant restraint, harm, and agony, redeeming the people with himself so they might be saved thereby from the punishment of the Hereafter and eternal perdition. The Christians have made this word—meaning the issue of the crucifixion and redemption (الفداء) — *the act of delivering from sin through sacrifice* — the foundation of their mission; they neither begin except with it, nor conclude except upon it. This is while the Quran makes the foundation of the Islamic mission monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — as Allah said, addressing His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): **قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — *Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists’* (12:108). This is despite the fact that the Messiah (according to what the Gospels state, as previously cited) used to make the first of the commandments monotheism (التوحيد) and the love of Allah, the Glorified.

Other Muslims and researchers have debated them regarding the aspects of corruption and falsehood contained in this claim of theirs. Books and treatises have been authored, and pages and scrolls filled with the explanation of its contradiction to the necessity of reason (ضرورة العقل) and its inconsistency with the books of the Two Testaments. What concerns us and accords with the purpose for which this book was established is the explanation of the aspects of its contradiction to the principles of the Quranic teaching, concluding with an explanation of the difference between what the Quran establishes of intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — and what they establish of redemption (الفداء).

Furthermore, the Quran explicitly mentions that it only addresses people and speaks to them by explaining what is near the horizon of their intellects (عقولهم) and what its explanations can reach of their comprehension and understanding. This is the matter by which man distinguishes truth (الحق) from falsehood (الباطل), thus submitting to the former and rejecting the latter, and differentiates between good and evil, and the beneficial and the harmful, thus taking the former and leaving the latter. What we have mentioned regarding the Quran's consideration of the judgment of sound reason (العقل السليم) in its explanations is something beyond doubt for anyone who refers to the Mighty Book.

As for what they mentioned, it contains **firstly:** their mention of the disobedience (معصية) of Adam (عليه‌السلام) by eating from the forbidden tree. The Quran refutes this from two perspectives:

The first: that the prohibition there was an advisory prohibition (نهياً إرشادياً) intended for the welfare of the one prohibited and the direction of rectitude (الرشد) in his affair, not the exercise of mastership (المولوية). A command of this nature does not result in reward or masterly punishment (عقاب مولوي) upon its fulfillment or abandonment, like the commands and prohibitions of an advisor to the one who consults him, or the commands and prohibitions of a doctor to a patient. Rather, what results from the fulfillment of an advisory obligation is the rectitude intended for the benefit of the subject, and from its violation, falling into the corruption of the violation and the harm of the act insofar as it is an act. In short, nothing befell Adam (عليه‌السلام) except that he was removed from Paradise and missed the comfort of proximity and the joy of [divine] pleasure. As for the punishment of the Hereafter, no, because he did not commit a masterly disobedience (معصية مولوية) such that it would entail punishment. Refer to the commentary (تفسير) of verses 35–39 of Surah al-Baqarah.

The second: that he (عليه‌السلام) was a prophet, and the Quran sanctifies the station of the prophets (عليهم السلام) and absolves their noble souls from committing acts of disobedience or transgressing (الفسق) the command of Allah, the Glorified. Rational proof (البرهان العقلي) also supports this; refer to what we mentioned in the discussion on the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets in the commentary of verse 213 of Surah al-Baqarah.

**Secondly:** their saying that the sin (الخطيئة) became attached to Adam. The Quran refutes this with His saying: **ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ** — *Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him* (20:122), and His saying: **فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ** — *Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful* (2:37).

Rational consideration supports this, rather it clarifies it; for sin and the consequence of guilt (تبعة الذنب) are only a cautioned and feared matter that reason or the Master considers necessary for violation and rebellion to solidify the matter of obligation (التكليف). Were it not for punishment and reward, the matter of mastership (المولوية) would not be established, and neither command nor prohibition would be obeyed. Just as it is among the functions of mastership to extend punishment over criminals for their crimes, like reward for the obedient for their acts of obedience, it is also among the functions of mastership to have absolute discretion within the sphere of its mastership. Thus, the Master has the right to overlook the sin of the sinners and the disobedience of the disobedient through pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة), for it is a type of discretion and governance (حكومة), just as He has the right to take them to account for it, which is also a type of governance. The goodness of pardon and forgiveness from masters and those of power and authority is generally beyond doubt, and the rational among humans have employed it until this time. Therefore, the notion that every sin originating from a human is necessarily attached to the human is entirely groundless; otherwise, the principle of pardon and forgiveness would have no reality. For forgiveness and pardon only exist to erase the sin and nullify the effect of the guilt. Given the assumption that the sin is necessarily attached and inseparable, no subject remains for pardon and forgiveness. This is while Divine Revelation is filled with the discourse of pardon and forgiveness, and the books of the Two Testaments are likewise, such that even this speech quoted from them is not devoid of it. In short, the claim that a sin among sins or an error among errors is necessarily attached and inherently incapable of being forgiven or erased—even through repentance (التوبة), turning back (الإنابة), return, and remorse—is something that neither sound reason nor a straight nature (طبع مستقيم) accepts.

**Thirdly:** their saying that Adam's sin, just as it was attached to him, was likewise attached to his progeny until the Day of Resurrection, necessitates that the consequence of a guilt originating from one person also encompasses others who did not sin in terms of masterly disobediences. In other words, that an act originates from one person and its disobedience and consequence generalize to someone other than its doer just as it encompasses its doer. This is different from a group committing a disobedience and others from their successors being pleased with it, such that the disobedience is counted against all. In short, it is the bearing of a burden (الوزر) without the commission of the guilt, and the Quran rejects this as in His saying: **أَلَّا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ** — *that no bearer shall bear another’s burden* — **وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ** — *and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for* (53:38–39). Reason supports this due to the ugliness of punishing one who did not sin for a guilt that did not originate from him. Refer to the discussions on acts (الأفعال) in the commentary of verses 216–218 of Surah al-Baqarah.

**Fourthly:** their discourse is based on the consequence of all sins and guilts being eternal perdition (الهلاك الأبدي) without distinction between them. Its necessity is that sins and guilts do not differ in terms of smallness or greatness; rather, all of them would be major, destructive sins (كبائر موبقات). What the Noble Quran views in its teaching is that sins and disobediences are diverse: among them are major sins (كبائر), and among them are minor sins (صغائر); among them are those reached by forgiveness, and among them are those not reached except through repentance, such as polytheism (الشرك). The Exalted said: **إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ** — *If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds* (4:31). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ** — *Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes* (4:48). Thus, the Exalted made among the forbidden prohibited things—which are the sins and guilts—those that are major sins, and those that are misdeeds (سيئات), meaning minor sins, by the evidence of juxtaposition. And the Exalted made among the guilts those that do not accept forgiveness, and those that do. Thus, guilts are diverse in any case, and not every guilt necessitates abiding in the Fire and eternal perdition.

Furthermore, reason rejects the arrangement of all sins and their organization into a single string; for a slap is not like killing, and a suspicious look is not like adultery, and so on. The rational among humans in all eras have not placed every sin and error in the place of another, and they see for different disobediences different consequences and accountings. How then can it be correct to treat all of them in one way despite this glaring difference between them? If their difference is assumed, it would only be correct to assign eternal punishment and eternal perdition to some of them, such as polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, as the Noble Quran says. It is well known that violating a certain prohibition in eating from the tree does not take the place of disbelief (الكفر) in Allah the Great and what resembles that; thus, there is no reason to make its punishment and consequence eternal torment. (Refer to the previously mentioned discussion on acts).

**Fifthly:** what they mentioned regarding the occurrence of the problem and the emergence of conflict between the attribute of mercy (الرحمة) and the attribute of justice (العدل), then the stratagem to remove it by the descent of the Messiah and his ascent in the manner they described. One who reflects on this discourse and the necessities it entails finds that they view Allah—Exalted and Sanctified is He—as an existing Creator to Whom this created world with all its parts is attributed and terminates; however, He only acts by will (إرادة) and knowledge (علم) within Himself, and His will in its realization depends on a cognitive preference (ترجيح علمي), just as a human only wills something when he prefers it by his knowledge. Thus, there are interests (مصالح) and corruptions (مفاسد) upon which Allah applies His acts and thus performs them. He might err in the application and thus regret (1) the act; He might think about a matter and not be guided to the way of its rectitude; He might be ignorant of a matter. In short, He, the Exalted, in His attributes and acts, is like a human; He only does what He does through reflection and deliberation, seeking therein to apply His act to the interest. Thus, He is governed by the judgment of interests and overcome by their operation upon Him from the outside. It is possible for Him to be guided to rectitude, and it is possible for Him to stray, err, or be heedless; thus, He sometimes knows and sometimes is ignorant, and He sometimes overcomes and is sometimes overcome. His power (قدرته) is limited like His knowledge. If this which was mentioned is permissible for Him, then all else that befalls a reflecting, willing agent in his act—such as joy, sadness, praise, regret, elation, emotion, and so on—is also permissible for Him. One who is of this state would be a material (مادياً), corporeal (جسمانياً) being, falling under the law of motion (الحركة), change (التغير), and perfection-seeking (الاستكمال). One who is such is a contingent (ممكن), created being, rather a manufactured human, and not the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted, the Creator of everything.

**And you**, by referring to the books of the Two Testaments, will find the truth of all that we have attributed to them regarding the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted, in terms of His corporeality and His being characterized by all the attributes of corporeal beings, especially humans.

The Quran, in all these mentioned meanings, sanctifies Allah, the Exalted, from these superstitious delusions; as the Exalted says: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ** — *Immaculate is Allah from what they attribute [to Him]* (37:159). Decisive rational proofs (البراهين العقلية) are established that He, the Exalted, is an Essence (ذات) combining all attributes of perfection; thus, He has existence (الوجود) without any taint of non-existence (عدم),

absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) without any taint of weakness, absolute knowledge (العلم المطلق) without the occurrence of ignorance, and absolute life (الحيوة المطلقة) without the possibility of death or annihilation. If He is such, it is not permissible for a change of state to occur in His existence, knowledge, power, or life.

If He is neither a body nor corporeal, then different states and various occurrences do not befall Him: such as heedlessness, oversight, error, regret, confusion, being affected, emotion, humiliation, smallness, being overcome, and the like. We have fully provided the demonstrative research related to these meanings in this book in appropriate places; the researcher will find them if he refers to them.

The discerning critic and the reflecting contemplator must compare the two views: what the Noble Quran says regarding the God of the world—thus establishing for Him every attribute of perfection and sanctifying Him from every attribute of deficiency, and ultimately considering Him greater and more magnificent than for our understandings to judge Him with what they have accompanied from the world of limitation and measure; and what the Two Testaments establish regarding the Creator, the Exalted, which is not found except in the myths of Greece and the superstitions of ancient India and China, and matters that early man used to imagine and thus be affected by what his imagination presented to him.

**Sixthly:** their saying that Allah sent His son, the Messiah, and commanded him to enter one of the wombs to be born as a human while being God. This is the irrational claim for the explanation of whose falsehood the Noble Quran arose, as we clarified in the previous explanation, so we shall not repeat it.

It is well known that reason also does not support it; for if you reflect on the attributes that must be said to characterize the Necessary (الواجب), the Exalted—such as eternal stability, lack of change, lack of limitation of existence, encompassment of everything, and sanctification from time and space and what follows them—and you reflect on the formation of a human from the time of being a drop of fluid, then a fetus in a womb—whether you consider in its meaning the interpretation of the Melkites for this word, or the interpretation of the Nestorians, or the interpretation of the Jacobites or others—there is no proportion between that which has corporeality and all the attributes and effects of corporeality, and that in which there is no corporeality nor anything of what it is characterized by, such as time, space, motion, or otherwise. How then can the union (الاتحاد) between them be conceived in any way?

The lack of correspondence of the mentioned claim with necessary rational propositions (القضايا الضرورية العقلية) is the secret behind what Paul and others among their saintly leaders mention regarding the disparagement of philosophy and the belittling of rational judgments. Paul says: "For it is written, 'I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.' Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this world? Hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world? ... For the Jews require a sign, and the Greeks seek after wisdom: But we preach (1) Christ crucified" (Epistle of Paul, Chapter 1). Similar words are many in his speech and the speech of others, and they are only for the policy of dissemination, broadcasting, preaching, and exhortation; one who directs his gaze to these epistles and books and delves into their method of addressing people and delivering their explanations to them will be certain of this.

From what has passed, it becomes clear what is in their saying: that He, the Exalted, is infallible (معصوم) from sins and guilts; for the God they have depicted is not protected from error at all, in the sense of error in perception and error in action, without it terminating in the violation of one whose agreement is mandatory.

As for sin (الذنب) and disobedience (المعصية) in the sense of rebellion in what obedience and submission are mandatory, it is inconceivable in His right, the Exalted; thus, infallibility (العصمة) is also inconceivable in His right, the Glorified.

**Seventhly:** their saying that after he became a human, he socialized with people as a human socializes with a human, until he was surrendered to his enemies. In this is the permission for the Necessary (الواجب) to be characterized by one of the realities (حقائق) of contingent beings (الممكنات), such that he would be God and man in the same rank. Thus, it would be permissible for the Necessary to become something from His creatures—meaning to be characterized by the reality of every species among these external species. So, at one time He would be a human among humans, at another a horse, at another a bird, at another an insect, at another something else, and at another He would be more than one species among the species, such as a human, a horse, and an insect together.

Likewise, it would be permissible for any assumed act among the acts of existing things to originate from Him, due to the permissibility of Him becoming that species and thus performing its specific act. Similarly, it would be permissible for contradictory acts to originate from Him together, such as justice and injustice, and for Him to be characterized by contradictory attributes, such as knowledge and ignorance, power and weakness, life and death, and wealth and poverty. Exalted is the King, the Truth; and this is other than the previously mentioned difficulty in the sixth point.
